chief_complaint: my period cramps are so bad I feel faint

med_suggestions.dosing: 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.sig: 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.notes: NSAIDs are first-line for dysmenorrhea if no contraindications.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.

med_suggestions.dosing: 500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.sig: 500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.

med_suggestions.dosing: 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
med_suggestions.sig: 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
med_suggestions.notes: Use when NSAIDs contraindicated; may be less effective alone.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Severe liver disease; heavy alcohol use; allergy.

note.soap:
S:
The patient is a 23-year-old female presenting with severe menstrual cramps accompanied by heavy bleeding and episodes of feeling faint. She reports no unilateral pain. There are no reported allergies. Pregnancy is not a concern. Patient is not pregnant.

O:
No physical examination or vital signs available due to phone-only consultation. No evidence of ectopic pregnancy or other red flags reported.

A:
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Severe primary dysmenorrhea with heavy menstrual bleeding causing presyncope.
- Differential:
  1. Secondary dysmenorrhea (e.g., endometriosis, fibroids)
  2. Anemia secondary to heavy menstrual bleeding
  3. Coagulopathy contributing to heavy bleeding
  4. Other gynecologic pathology (e.g., pelvic inflammatory disease)
- Red flags considered: Heavy bleeding with faintness (considered), no ectopic pregnancy signs reported.

P:
- Disposition: Urgent care/primary care follow-up recommended for physical examination and further evaluation.
- Plan: Ibuprofen — 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course). Alternative: Naproxen (500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)).
  Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
  - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
  - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
  - Ibuprofen — 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course)
    - Notes: NSAIDs are first-line for dysmenorrhea if no contraindications.
    - Exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.
  - Naproxen — 500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)
    - Notes: Alternative NSAID option.
    - Exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.
  - Acetaminophen — 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
    - Notes: Use when NSAIDs contraindicated; may be less effective alone.
    - Exclusions: Severe liver disease; heavy alcohol use; allergy.
  - Supportive care:
  - Non-pharmacologic: Heat pack to lower abdomen; gentle activity/stretching; hydration.
  - Assess pregnancy possibility: If pregnancy is possible or period is abnormal for patient, consider pregnancy evaluation rather than assuming dysmenorrhea.
  - Follow-up and safety:
  - Re-evaluation: If severe pain is new, progressively worsening, associated with heavy bleeding, syncope, fever, pelvic pain between cycles, or concern for secondary dysmenorrhea—needs evaluation.
  - Urgent/ED now: Fainting, severe one-sided pelvic pain, suspected pregnancy/ectopic, soaking >1 pad/hour, severe weakness, or signs of shock.
  - Recommend initiation of NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 6-8 hours with food) to manage menstrual pain.
  - Consider iron studies and complete blood count if heavy bleeding persists or anemia symptoms worsen.
  - Advise patient to monitor bleeding amount and symptoms of worsening faintness or dizziness.
  - If symptoms worsen or new symptoms develop (e.g., severe pelvic pain, fever, prolonged heavy bleeding), seek immediate medical attention.
- Safety net: Instruct patient to seek emergency care if she experiences severe weakness, persistent fainting, chest pain, or severe abdominal pain.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.

evidence.title: ACOG Committee Opinion: Dysmenorrhea and Endometriosis in the Adolescent (2018)
PubMed: ACOG Committee Opinion No. 760 (citation record)
